paper_title: DC-Solver: Improving Predictor-Corrector Diffusion Sampler via Dynamic  Compensation
paper_abstract:
Diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) have shown remarkable performance in
visual synthesis but are computationally expensive due to the need for multiple
evaluations during the sampling. Recent predictor-corrector diffusion samplers
have significantly reduced the required number of function evaluations (NFE),
but inherently suffer from a misalignment issue caused by the extra corrector
step, especially with a large classifier-free guidance scale (CFG). In this
paper, we introduce a new fast DPM sampler called DC-Solver, which leverages
dynamic compensation (DC) to mitigate the misalignment of the
predictor-corrector samplers. The dynamic compensation is controlled by
compensation ratios that are adaptive to the sampling steps and can be
optimized on only 10 datapoints by pushing the sampling trajectory toward a
ground truth trajectory. We further propose a cascade polynomial regression
(CPR) which can instantly predict the compensation ratios on unseen sampling
configurations. Additionally, we find that the proposed dynamic compensation
can also serve as a plug-and-play module to boost the performance of
predictor-only samplers. Extensive experiments on both unconditional sampling
and conditional sampling demonstrate that our DC-Solver can consistently
improve the sampling quality over previous methods on different DPMs with a
wide range of resolutions up to 1024$\times$1024. Notably, we achieve 10.38 FID
(NFE=5) on unconditional FFHQ and 0.394 MSE (NFE=5, CFG=7.5) on
Stable-Diffusion-2.1. Code is available at https://github.com/wl-zhao/DC-Solver

findings: Misalignment Issue in Predictor-Corrector Samplers: Existing predictor-corrector DPM samplers (e.g., UniPC) perform a corrector step to refine x_{t_i}, but then buffer ε_θ(x_{t_i}, t_i) rather than ε_θ(x_{t_i}^c, t_i). This misalignment between the buffered model output and the corrected state degrades sampling quality, especially under large classifier-free guidance scales. Empirical analysis shows that re-computing ε_θ at the corrected state improves results but doubles network evaluations.

Convergence Preservation of Dynamic Compensation: A theoretical result proving that applying Dynamic Compensation to a p-th order predictor-only or (p+1)-th order predictor-corrector DPM sampler does not lower its global convergence order. Under mild Lipschitz and step-size assumptions, the local truncation error remains O(h^{p+1}) and the global error remains O(h^p) (or O(h^{p+1}) for predictor-corrector), guaranteeing that DC-Solver preserves the original sampler’s high-order accuracy.

Dynamic Compensation for Predictor-Only Solvers: An empirical finding that Dynamic Compensation can be applied as a plug-and-play enhancement to predictor-only DPM samplers such as DDIM and DPM-Solver++. By searching stepwise compensation ratios and interpolating past model outputs, even these single-stage solvers see dramatic FID improvements (e.g., DDIM drops from 57.92 to 16.56 at 5 NFE), indicating the broad applicability of the DC module.

code_overview:
## Dependencies
- PyTorch
- Stable-Diffusion or diffusers codebase
- Standard scientific Python stack (NumPy, etc.)

techniques.name: DC-Solver
techniques.description:
An end-to-end fast sampling algorithm for diffusion probabilistic models (DPMs) that integrates a predictor-corrector framework with Dynamic Compensation to mitigate the misalignment between corrected states and model outputs. DC-Solver proceeds as follows:

1. Precompute or predict a sequence of compensation ratios {ρ_i*} for i=0…M−1 via either the searching algorithm (Algorithm 1) or Cascade Polynomial Regression (Algorithm 2).
2. Initialize the buffer Q with the most recent K model outputs ε_θ(x_{t_{i−k}}, t_{i−k}).
3. For each sampling step i from 0 to M−1:
   a. If i ≥ K, compute the compensated model output:
      • ˆε^{ρ_i*}(x_{t_i}^c, t_i) via Lagrange interpolation (Dynamic Compensation) using Q and ratio ρ_i*.
      • Replace the last entry of Q with ˆε^{ρ_i*}.
   b. Apply the predictor step Pred(x_{t_i}^c, Q) to obtain x_{t_{i+1}}.
   c. Apply the corrector step Corr(x_{t_{i+1}}, ε_θ(x_{t_i}, t_i), Q) to obtain the refined x_{t_{i+1}}^c.
   d. Append ε_θ(x_{t_{i+1}}, t_{i+1}) to Q and remove the oldest buffer entry.
4. Return x_{t_M}^c as the final sample.

By dynamically compensating the buffered model outputs and using learned or predicted compensation ratios, DC-Solver achieves high sample quality with very few function evaluations.

components.name: Dynamic Compensation
components.description:
A method to approximate the denoising network output at a corrected state without extra model evaluations. Given the corrected intermediate sample x_{t_i}^c, a compensation ratio ρ_i, and a buffer Q of K+1 past model outputs {ε_θ(x_{t_{i−k}}, t_{i−k})}_{k=0}^K, Dynamic Compensation computes

  1. t_i' = ρ_i · t_i + (1−ρ_i) · t_{i−1}
  2. ˆε^{ρ_i}(x_{t_i}^c, t_i) = Σ_{k=0}^K [∏_{l≠k} (t_i'−t_{i−l})/(t_{i−k}−t_{i−l})] · ε_θ(x_{t_{i−k}}, t_{i−k})

This Lagrange interpolation over the past K+1 timesteps yields an estimate of ε_θ at the corrected state, which replaces the last entry in Q to better align future predictor steps.

components.name: Searching for Compensation Ratios
components.description:
An optimization procedure (Algorithm 1) that fits a per-step scalar compensation ratio ρ_i* by minimizing the squared L₂ distance between the next-step corrected sample and a ground-truth trajectory. Given N training noise seeds, ground-truth trajectory x_t^{GT,N}, current buffer Q, and corrected intermediate x_{t_i}^c,N:

  1. Initialize ρ_i=1.0 and copy Q to Q^{copy}.
  2. For ℓ=1 to L:
     a. Compute ˆε^{ρ_i} via Dynamic Compensation using Q^{copy}.
     b. Form Q^{ρ_i} = [Q^{copy}_{[:-1]}, ˆε^{ρ_i}].
     c. Run one predictor-corrector step to obtain x_{t_{i+1}}^{c,N}.
     d. Compute gradient ∇_{ρ_i} ||x_{t_{i+1}}^{c,N} − x_{t_{i+1}}^{GT,N}||²₂ and update ρ_i ← ρ_i − α ∇_{ρ_i}.
  3. Return the optimized ρ_i* and updated Q^{ρ_i*}.

This search takes under 5 minutes on 10 datapoints and produces compensation ratios that generalize to unseen noises.

components.name: Cascade Polynomial Regression
components.description:
A lightweight regression scheme to predict optimal compensation ratios for arbitrary numbers of function evaluations (NFE) and classifier-free guidance scales (CFG) without re-searching. It fits three cascaded polynomials:

  1. f₁(NFE | φ^{(1)}_{j,k}) of order p₁ to model how coefficients vary with NFE.
  2. f₂(CFG | φ^{(2)}_{j}) of order p₂ to model how those coefficients vary with CFG.
  3. f₃(i | φ^{(3)}) of order p₃ to produce ρ_i* as a function of the step index i.

In practice, one collects optimal ratios for a small grid of (NFE, CFG) via the search algorithm, uses curve_fit to estimate φ^{(1)}, φ^{(2)}, φ^{(3)}, and at inference computes ρ_i* = f₃(i|φ^{(3)}) instantly for any new NFE/CFG.
